_note: Static, rule-based dismantle text for Level 4's second beat. Each block re-frames one of the four overreach claim types with a short, plain-English breakdown of how thin the actual evidence is. Author voice: a friend who actually read the papers, not a researcher writing for other researchers.

big_five.title: Big Five
big_five.summary: Researchers have tried to predict the Big Five from things like phone use and short games. The best they've done lines up with about 3 to 5 percent of the actual variation between people. That's enough to publish a paper, way too thin to call this a portrait of anyone.
citations: Stachl et al. (2020), PNAS — smartphone behavior predicts the Big Five at correlations of roughly 0.10 to 0.25.
Yarkoni & Westfall (2017), Perspectives on Psychological Science — when you actually test these models on new people, they hold up much less than the original headlines suggest.

political_values.title: Politics
political_values.summary: Nobody has shown that 15 minutes of gameplay tells you anything real about how someone votes. The paragraph above sounds confident because it borrows the shape of a real read. There's no evidence underneath it.
citations: Iyer et al. (2012), PLOS ONE — political leanings correlate with moral foundations on long surveys. They do not correlate with quick decision games.

life_history.title: Your life
life_history.summary: There's no way to tell whether you're 24 or 54, partnered or single, college-educated or not, from how you played a short game. Brokers sell exactly this kind of guess every day under names like 'lifestyle enrichment' — confident sentences wearing a suit.
citations: Christl & Spiekermann (2016), Networks of Control — overview of how brokers assemble and sell this kind of guessed-at lifestyle data.

consumer_profile.title: What you'd buy
consumer_profile.summary: This is the easiest one to sell and the one brokers actually make money on. Thousands of segments per person, fractions of a cent each, packaged for ad networks. The paragraph above is roughly what's for sale right now.
citations: FTC (2014), Data Brokers: A Call for Transparency and Accountability — the canonical look at what's in these catalogs.
Crain (2018), New Media & Society — how the consumer-segment market actually works behind the scenes.

closing: This is the gap. Fluent inference on one side, thin evidence on the other. The whole profiling industry lives in that gap, getting paid to sound confident anyway.
